paragraphs.body: I am Brittany Keller, and I am a sophomore at the University of Pittsburgh majoring in the pre-physician assistant track. I recently read your essay, “Girl,” and I found it very interesting, especially from being a girl while growing up in today’s society. Your writing effectively explains how to be a stay-at-home mom, and how much pressure society can put on you as a girl. In your writing, I noticed that it was a different structure and how you did not use any punctuation until the end when you put a question mark, so I saw it as a long one-sided conversation you had with your daughter. In addition, I found it very engaging because you used various descriptive words throughout that let me create an image in my mind during this. For example, the words benna, wharf-rat boys, slut are all eye-catching because I feel like the idea of where you're going relates a lot to our generation right now. Not wanting your daughter to grow up around negativity and bad energy is always important and that makes a great parent! I related this to my own life because my parents always made sure to teach me things around the house and to be street smart. To this day, I thank them consistently for who I am and how they raised me because you can always tell if a person was raised well or not. I feel like as a girl today it can feel overwhelming because there are a lot of responsibilities to take on, especially staying safe and succeeding. What made you choose to write this with no punctuation? Are you using what you learned while growing up to better her? Thank you so much for sharing this essay because not many people think the way you do and realize our generation, and I loved the way you structured your writing!

In addition, I found it very engaging because you used various descriptive words throughout that let me create an image in my mind during this. For example, the words benna, wharf-rat boys, slut are all eye-catching

annotations.comment: I feel as though this is at the heart of your communication with the author, and would love to know a little more about your own experience--could you include a specific lesson that they taught you? Just one example could bolster the strength of the generalization and give the author a better sense of who you are.

annotations.comment: You're doing some really nice work here. While it is often difficult to communicate an author's ideas back to them without sounding like the writing is forced, you're doing a great job of balancing a summary of her work with your own thoughts about it. I truly would love to see you be a little more specific about your own experiences, so Kincaid could get a better sense of you, personally.
